focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Less than an hour after Lucie called her Jeanne at the trapdoor, the packet of notes rode flat and hot inside Clemence's stays. The clean smell of boiled linen still clung to Elise's hands, already giving way to dust and horse piss as they walked. Elise could tell where it sat by the way Clemence kept one forearm folded over her middle as they walked, guarding it with the same practical instinct she used to shield bread from rain. The paper bundle in Nadir's arms had taken the shape of his body already. He carried it turned a little sideways, as if he were making room for a blow that had not yet arrived, and every few streets he checked the inside pocket of his coat with the back of his ink-black fingers. A corner of folded proof stuck there. Lucie had begged him for a fox before they left the quarry, and he had promised one with a seriousness he would have mocked in anybody else.

Ferrand had come because Ferrand came where movement and money met. He said it was because he knew the lane and because a man looked less suspicious than two women and a compositor carrying wrapped stock. Elise had not corrected the count. Ferrand always spoke as if the world were already arranged to flatter him. Cheap cologne leaked off him in warm bursts when the wind shifted, trying and failing to cover a night's sweat. He had shaved in a hurry. One patch under his jaw still showed the dark rasp, and the knuckles on his right hand were swollen old white where they had once broken and healed badly.

Late spring had softened the city without cleaning it. Cart ruts held old rain and horse piss. Laundry snapped from upper windows over a butcher's blue flies. They were south enough now that the streets widened and thinned in uncertain patches, Paris fraying into Montrouge with yard...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...fuge had its vanity as well as its care. She had done it because the book in that niche contained bread beside fuse, shelter beside theater, and Vautrin would flatten all of it into the same charge without losing a minute's sleep.

Elise picked up the lamp and started back.

At the last turn before the sleeping gallery she could hear Lucien again, quieter now, reading something aloud to Lucie because the child would not sleep after the quarrel. His voice came braided with coughs and patience. Clemence interrupted now and then to correct a hem or a fact. Somewhere beyond them Abel was either with Ferrand or thinking about him. The choice in the wall sat between all those sounds like another hidden mouth.

When Elise reached the schoolroom, nobody looked up at once. That spared her.

She set the lamp down by the onions and said, "The small ones were under the basket."

Clemence grunted.

Lucie held out a scrap of paper on which she had written her own name badly and with full seriousness. Lucien was helping her shape the last letter with one ink-dark finger. Elise looked at the child's bent head, at the chalk wall, at the stocking Clemence had gone back to darning, at the empty patch of floor where Ferrand's satchel had rested.

Vautrin would get no ledger from her in the morning.

He would get something thinner, if he got anything at all.

And if he noticed the difference, if he put his neat hands on the missing weight and followed the absence back to her, then the cost had already begun. The book was in the wall. Abel was in Ferrand's reach. Wednesday was coming whether she fed the machine or not.

She sat down beside Lucie and took up the needle Clemence had left on the bench, because morning would come with baskets, wet linen, and no mercy for what the night had said.
